sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 Minor injuries include superficial wounds, abrasions, minor lacerations, contusions, sprains, and simple fractures. These injuries are not immediately life-threatening but require appropriate assessment and management to prevent complications such as infection, further tissue damage, or functional loss.

 **Epistaxis:**
 Epistaxis, or nosebleed, is bleeding from the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, or nostrils. It is common, affecting up to 60% of people at least once in their lifetime. Most cases are benign and self-limiting, but severe or recurrent episodes may indicate underlying pathology or require advanced intervention

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 - Localized pain, swelling, or tenderness
- Bruising (contusion)
- Superficial bleeding (abrasion or laceration)
- Limited range of motion (sprain)
- Mild deformity (simple fracture)
- No signs of shock or major trauma

 **Epistaxis:**
 - Bleeding from one or both nostrils
- Blood in the throat or mouth
- Swallowing or spitting blood
- Nasal congestion or blockage
- Dizziness or light-headedness (if blood loss is significant)
- Pallor or signs of shock (rare, in severe cases
- Nausea and Vomiting (frank blood)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Aseptic technique should be used in the treatment of all open wounds
- Swelling, bruising, and deformity are classic signs of fracture. Severe sprains can present similarly and should be treated cautiously
- In epistaxis, consider anticoagulant use, recent trauma, recurrent bleeding, or posterior bleeding
- Patients with posterior epistaxis may bleed more than is externally visible
- Topical tranexamic acid may be a useful adjunct in persistent anterior epistaxis where available and clinically appropriate
- Do not pack both nostrils simultaneously unless the airway is secured

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey

**Wound care:**
- Control external bleeding with direct pressure
- Clean wounds with sterile water or other appropriate solution
- Remove visible superficial debris only
- Cover wounds with a suitable dressing

**Sprains/strains:**
- Consider compression where appropriate
- Elevate the injury where practicable
- Consider intermittent cooling
- Advise rest and follow-up where available

**Simple fracture:**
- Splint simple fractures

**Epistaxis:**
- Sit the patient upright and leaning slightly forward
- Discourage swallowing blood to reduce nausea and vomiting
- Apply firm pressure to the soft part of the nose continuously for at least 10 minutes
- Consider a bolster or towel beneath the nose to collect blood
- Consider a topical vasoconstrictor if stocked and appropriate
- If bleeding persists, consider anterior packing or topical TXA in consultation with the on-call doctor where appropriate

- Consider simple analgesia; avoid NSAIDs during active bleeding
- Assess for distal neurovascular compromise where appropriate
- Record vital signs
- Consider secondary survey
- Enquire about tetanus status where relevant

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
**Wound care:**
- Consider Steri-strips, wound glue, or suturing where appropriate

**Epistaxis:**
- If direct pressure has failed, consider anterior nasal packing
- Consider applying topical TXA to packing material in consultation with the on-call doctor
- If bleeding persists despite anterior measures, suspect posterior bleeding and escalate
- Consider analgesia

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Lithium dust, commonly generated during mining, can cause irritation and inflammation of the nasal passages, potentially leading to epistaxis
- Epistaxis Patient education on prevention: humidification, gentle nose care, avoid trauma
